coredata.dc:title: Prussian blue nanoparticles-enabled sensitive and accurate ratiometric fluorescence immunoassay for histamine
coredata.dc:creator: Liao, Cai-Xia
Jia, Bao-Zhu
Wang, Hong
Sun, Yuan-Ming
Xu, Xiao-Yan
Wei, Xiao-Qun
Shen, Yu-Dong
Lei, Hong-Tao
Xu, Zhen-Lin
Luo, Lin
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a nanozyme-mediated ratiometric fluorescence immunoassay for histamine (HA) has been developed. Prussian blue nanoparticles (PBNPs) with outstanding peroxidase-like activity were labelled with goat anti-mouse IgG via a facile electrostatic adsorption to yield the nanozyme-antibody conjugate which acted as a bridge to link the ratiometric fluorescence readout with HA concentration. As substrate, o-phenylenediamine (OPD) was oxidized into 2,3-diaminophenazine (oxOPD) by H2O2 under the catalysis of PBNPs, producing a novel emission at 570 nm and quenching the fluorescence of carbon dots (CDs) at 450 nm simultaneously. Under optimal conditions, the ratio of fluorescence intensity at 570 nm and 450 nm (I570/I450) linearly correlated with HA concentration ranging from 1.6 ng/mL to 125 μg/mL, with a detection limit (LOD) of 1.2 ng/mL. In addition, analytical performances including specificity, accuracy and applicability were evaluated, which revealed that this ratiometric fluorescence immunoassay affords an effective platform for sensitive and accurate detection of HA.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescence immunoassay
Prussian blue nanoparticles
Nanozyme
Histamine

Characterizations of PBNPs, carbon dots, and PBNPs-antibody conjugate
Feasibility of PBNP-mediated ratiometric fluorescence response system
Analytical performance of the ratiometric fluorescence immunoassay

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (A) EDX spectrum (B) of the prepared PBNPs, TEM image (C) and high-resolution TEM image (D) of CDs. The inset shows size distribution of CDs and the optical photos of CDs under sunlight and UV lamp (E) Characterization of PBNPs-Ab2 by SDS PAGE. (F) Zeta potential of the prepared PBNPs and PBNPs-Ab2.

Plots of steady-state kinetic assay of PBNPs with (A) the concentration of H2O2 (1.0 mM) fixed and OPD concentration varied and (B) the concentration of OPD (0.5 mM) fixed and the H2O2 concentration varied. (C, D) Lineweaver-Burk double reciprocal plots of activity of PBNPs with the concentration of one substrate fixed and the other varied.

(A) The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs (gray curve), CDs/OPD (wine curve), CD/H2O2 (dark cyan curve), CDs/PBNPs (orange curve), CDs/OPD/H2O2 (green curve), CDs/PBNPs/H2O2 (red curve), CDs/PBNPs/OPD (dark yellow curve), OPD/PBNPs/H2O2 (violet curve), and CDs/PBNPs/OPD/H2O2 (blue curve). (B) The fluorescence excitation (red curve) and emission (green curve) spectra of CDs and UV–vis spectra of oxOPD (black curve). (C) UV–vis spectra of CDs, oxOPD and CDs/oxOPD. (D) Fluorescence decay times of CDs/OPD/H2O2 (black curve) and CDs/OPD/H2O2/PBNPs (red curve).

(A) The fluorescence emission spectra of the ratiometric fluorescence immunoassay for various concentration of histamine (from 0.0016 to 125 μg/mL); (B) The calibration curve of fluorescence intensity ratio (I570/ I450) versus different levels of histamine, the inset was the linear plot of the ratiometric fluorescence ratio (I570/ I450) against the logarithm values of various concentration of histamine, error bar = RSD (n = 3).
Specificity of the ratiometric fluorescence immunoassay against histamine, histidine, tryptophane, tryptamine, tyramine and phenethylamine (spiked concentrations were 20 ng/mL).
Schematic illustration of PBNPs-mediated ratiometric fluorescence immunoassay for histamine.
Recoveries of histamine from different samples by the proposed ratiometric fluorescence immunoassay (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Prussian blue nanoparticles-enabled sensitive and accurate ratiometric fluorescence immunoassay for histamine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Safety/Guangdong Laboratory of Lingnan Modern Agriculture, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Safety/Guangdong Laboratory of Lingnan Modern Agriculture

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Safety/Guangdong Laboratory of Lingnan Modern Agriculture, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Biology and Food Engineering, Guangdong University of Education, Guangzhou 510303, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Biology and Food Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Biology and Food Engineering, Guangdong University of Education, Guangzhou 510303, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors are equally contributed to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a nanozyme-mediated ratiometric fluorescence immunoassay for histamine (HA) has been developed. Prussian blue nanoparticles (PBNPs) with outstanding peroxidase-like activity were labelled with goat anti-mouse IgG via a facile electrostatic adsorption to yield the nanozyme-antibody conjugate which acted as a bridge to link the ratiometric fluorescence readout with HA concentration. As substrate, o-phenylenediamine (OPD) was oxidized into 2,3-diaminophenazine (oxOPD) by H2O2 under the catalysis of PBNPs, producing a novel emission at 570 nm and quenching the fluorescence of carbon dots (CDs) at 450 nm simultaneously. Under optimal conditions, the ratio of fluorescence intensity at 570 nm and 450 nm (I570/I450) linearly correlated with HA concentration ranging from 1.6 ng/mL to 125 μg/mL, with a detection limit (LOD) of 1.2 ng/mL. In addition, analytical performances including specificity, accuracy and applicability were evaluated, which revealed that this ratiometric fluorescence immunoassay affords an effective platform for sensitive and accurate detection of HA.

ce:section.ce:para: Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), which combines the high-efficiency catalytic activity of the enzyme with the specific antigen–antibody binding reaction, has been extensively applied in food analysis, biomedical diagnosis and environmental monitoring for merits of simple operation, low cost, excellent specificity and high throughput (Song, Ye, Peng, Wang, & Niu, 2019; Wu, Ge, Yang, Hou, & Liu, 2015). However, the sensitivity and robustness of classic ELISA is compromised due to the its colorimetric readout and the susceptibility to inactivation of the enzyme used for antibody labeling (Jiao, Zhang, Du, Li, Yang, & Zhu, 2018). To date, considerable research has been conducted to enhance the performance of ELISA for more robust and reliable detection.

To circumvent the issue of inactivation of enzyme, many efforts have been invested to explore potential alternatives of natural enzymes for signal readout. Among them, nanomaterials with enzyme-mimicking activity, termed as nanozyme, had been demonstrated to possess merits such as, facile preparation, cost-effective and higher stability over natural enzyme (J. Wu et al., 2019). As a result, a variety of nanozymes including metal oxides (Fe3O4, CeO2, MnO2), noble metal (Au, Pt, Pd) nanomaterials, etc. have been employed to substitute horseradish peroxidase (HRP) to label antibody for development of modified ELISA (Cheng, Lin, Muhammad, Lin, & Wei, 2016; Gao et al., 2007; Jiang et al., 2016; Wan, Qi, Zhang, Wu, & Wang, 2012). However, noble metal-based nanozyme are expensive, the high cost and low abundance of precious metals hamper their extensive application. Prussian blue nanoparticles (PBNPs), as a kind of iron-based nanomaterial, has been demonstrated to possess higher peroxidase-mimicking activity than Fe3O4 NPs. Zhang et al. found that the kcat of PBNPs toward TMB was 4 times larger than that of Fe3O4 NPs (W. Zhang et al., 2016). Komkova et al reported that catalytically synthesized PBNPs display higher catalytic constant than natural enzyme peroxidase (Komkova, Karyakina, & Karyakin, 2018). Additionally, compared with nanozymes which need laborious modification and activation procedures to conjugate with antibodies, PBNPs can conjugate with antibody via an easy electrostatic adsorption method without additional activation (S. Liu et al., 2020). Therefore, PBNPs exhibits great potential as substitute of enzyme labels. Farka et al. had used PBNPs as a catalytic label and developed a robust sandwich nanozyme-linked immunosorbent assay (NLISA) for Salmonella Typhimurium. Compared with standard sandwich ELISA based on the same antibody, this NLISA shows higher stability and lower cost. However, since this NLISA was also based on colorimetric readout comparable sensitivity with standard ELISA was achieved.

For enhancing the sensitivity of ELISA, substantial research endeavors have been invested to seek other signal readouts beyond colorimetric readouts for antigen–antibody binding event, such as electrochemistry (Dong et al., 2017), fluorescence (Luo et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2020), and chemiluminescence (Zhang, Xiao, Zhang, & Wang, 2019). Among those strategies, fluorescence-based immunoassay has garnered considerable attentions because of the inherent simplicity, high sensitivity, and the emergence of many nanomaterials having excellent luminescent property (Zhou et al., 2020). To date, various nanomaterials such as, quantum dots (Wu et al., 2018), carbon dots (Wu, Wei, Pengpumkiat, Schumacher, & Remcho, 2015), MOFs (Wei, Zhang, Zhang, Ouyang, & Fu, 2019) have been employed for the development of highly sensitive fluorescent immunoassay. However, most of fluorescent immunoassays are dependent on the variations in monochromatic fluorescence intensity, which is liable to be interfered by exterior conditions, such as exciting light intensity, spectrophotometer efficiency and the fluorescent probe concentration (Tan, Zhang, Tang, Zheng, & Li, 2019). In contrast, ratiometric fluorescence assay by recording fluorescence intensity change at dual-wavelength possesses an intrinsic correction from false signals to achieve enhanced accuracy and sensitivity (Liu et al., 2019). Therefore, a variety of ratiometric fluorescence probes were constructed against various analytes, such as ions (Zhang, Wu, He, Xu, Li, & Ye, 2018), small molecules (Cue, Chen, & Yin, 2019) and biomolecules (Xu, Cen, Xu, Wei, Shi, & Hu, 2019), in recent years. However, integrating the ratiometric fluorescence strategy with immunoassay was seldom studied. Our group previously developed a highly sensitive ratiometric fluorescence immunoassay for sensitive quantification of ethyl carbamate using HRP-meidated oxidation of OPD and quenching of the fluorescent silicon nanoparticle (Luo et al., 2018). In addition, Liu et al. constructed a sensitive ratiometric fluorescence immunoassay for zearalenone using HRP-mediated quenching of luminescent gold-silver nanoclusters (Lv et al., 2019). However, the involvement of less stable HRP-labeled antibody confines the extensive applications for robust immunoassay.

Histamine (HA) is one the main causative agents responsible for food allergies and poisoning. It also acts as a key indicator for evaluate the quality and freshness of food (Gagic, Jamroz, Krizkova, Milosavljevic, Kopel, & Adam, 2019). Over the past years, a series of medical negligence cases associated with HA contamination occurred which were resulted from the intravenous injection of antibiotics generated through fermentation with fish peptone (Xu, Zhou, Eremin, Dias, & Zhang, 2020). Thus, development of rapid, accurate and reliable analytical method for HA monitoring is of great significance for guaranteeing food safety and human health (Li et al., 2020).

This work aimed at developing a performance-improved ELISA for HA detection by coupling the merits of nanozymes with ratiometric fluorescence assay. The immunoassay principle is presented in Scheme 1 . Carbon dots (CDs) are introduced as a reference fluorophore due to the distinctive characteristics including bright fluorescence, photostability, low toxicity, good biocompatibility (Biagioni, Capalbo, Lezzerini, & Pasero, 2014). After immunoreaction, PBNPs of the immune complex absorbed on the microplate catalyze the oxidation of nonfluorescent OPD by H2O2, producing an emission peak of oxOPD at 570 nm. Meanwhile, the formed oxOPD can efficiently quench the fluorescence of CDs at 450 nm via inner filter effect (IFE). Consequently, as the HA level in sample increases, the emission intensity at 570 nm decreased accompanied by the increase in emission intensity at 450 nm, realizing the quantification of HA via sensitive and accurate ratio-based fluorometric readout.

Potassium hexacyanoferrate (III) (K3[Fe(CN)6]) was purchased from Shantou Guanghua Chemical Co., Ltd (Shantou, China). Poly (vinylpyrrolidone) (PVP K30) was obtained from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). Ethylenediamine (EDA) and citrate were received from Aladdin Industrial Corporation. O-phenylenediamine (OPD) was acquired from Shanghai Linen Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Albumin Bovine V (BSA) was purchased from Beijing Biotopped Science & Technology Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Goat anti-mouse IgG (Ab2) was obtained from Sigma Aldrich (Shanghai, China). The antigen and monoclonal antibody (Ab1) against histamine were previously prepared in our lab (Luo et al., 2019). Unless otherwise specified, all other reagents used in this work were analytical grade. All aqueous solutions were prepared with ultrapure water throughout the work.

Immunoassay plates were rinsed with a microplate washer from Thermo Fisher (Shanghai, China), and the fluorescent measurements were recorded on a SpectraMax i3x Multiskan microplate reader obtained from Molecular Devices (MDC, USA). A steady state transient fluorescence spectrometer fls1000 purchased from Edinburgh company (Beijing, China) was used to measure the lifetime of carbon dots. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained from a Talos F200s microscope (FEI, Netherland). Ultrapure water was prepared by a Unique-s15 ultrapure water purifier (Guangzhou, China).

Carbon dots were synthesized as previously described with a slight modification (Zhu et al., 2013). Carbon dots were prepared as follows: citric acid (3.0 g) and ethylenediamine (600 μL) were mixed and dissolved into 30 mL of water under magnetic stirring. Then the mixture solution was transferred into a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (50 mL) and heated at 180 °C for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, carbon dots were obtained by dialyzing the resultant solution against distill water for 6 h.

PBNPs were prepared by reference to the procedures described previously with a slight modification (Wu, Wang, Zhou, Zhu, & Chen, 2021; Zhao et al., 2018). Briefly, PVP K30 (7.5 g, 0.75 mmol) and K3[Fe(CN)6] (283.5 mg, 0.86 mmol) were dissolved to100 mL of HCl (0.01 M). The mixture was stirred at room temperature for 1 h, yielding a clear solution. Then, the solution was refluxed at 80 °C for 20 h. When cooling to room temperature, the resultant blue dispersion was stored in 4 °C for further use.

As a kind of nanocrystalline coordination polymer with chemical formula Fe4[Fe(CN)6]3, PBNPs is of negative charge provided by –CN groups. Thus, the as-synthesized PBNPs can be conjugated with goat anti-mouse IgG (secondary antibody, Ab2) via an electrostatic adsorption method, which is a common strategy adopted to graft protein onto the PBNPs surface (Zhang, Gong, Zhang, Yang, Wang, & Gu, 2010; Zhao et al., 2018). Briefly, 7.5 μL of goat anti-mouse IgG (1.0 mg/mL) solution was added to 1 mL of PBNPs (0.4 mg/mL) solution. After vortex stirring for 1 min, the mixture solution was incubated at room temperature for 1 h. Next, 60 μL of BSA (10%) solution was added dropwise and kept at room temperature incubated for 1 h with agitation to block the residual sites on PBNPs surface. Then, the mixture solution was further incubated at 4 °C for 3 h, followed by a centrifugation (12000 r/min, 18 min) to separate the unconjugated protein, the precipitate was redissolved in 0.2 mL borate buffer and kept at −20 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Protocol of ratiometric fluorescence immunoassay for histamine
ce:section.ce:para: Fish, red wine, and yogurt samples were sampled from local market in Guangzhou, China, and the extract and pretreated procedures was as previously described (Luo et al., 2019). The analytical procedures were as follows: each well of black opaque microplates was first coated with 100 μL of HA-OVA (coating antigen) in bicarbonate buffer (pH 9.5, 0.05 M) overnight at 37 °C. After washing twice with PBST buffer (pH 7.4), 120 μL of blocking buffer was added and incubated for 3 h at 37 °C to block nonspecific sites. After removing the blocking buffer, the microplate was dried at 37 °C for 1 h. Then 50 μL of HA standard solution or sample extract and 50 μL of diluted anti-HA monoclonal antibody (Ab1) was added and incubated at 37 °C for 40 min, followed by a washing step with PBST buffer (five times). Then, 100 μL of diluted PBNPs- Ab2 was added and incubated for 30 min at 37 °C, followed by another washing step with PBST buffer. Next, the nanozyme-catalytic reaction was conducted as follows: 50 μL of OPD, 50 μL of H2O2 and 50 μL of CDs were added into the well. After incubation at 50 °C for 40 min, the florescence intensity at 450 nm (I450) and 570 nm (I570) under 400 nm excitation were measured, and ratio of I570 to I450 (I570/I450) was calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of PBNPs, carbon dots, and PBNPs-antibody conjugate
ce:section.ce:para: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of PBNPs (Fig. 1 A) showed that the PBNPs were of uniform cubic shape with average size of ∼ 50 nm. The energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) measurement of PBNPs indicated that the as-synthesized PBNPs contains C, N, Fe atom which was matched well with the element composition of typical PBNPs (Fig. 1B). TEM image (Fig. 1C) displayed the as-prepared CDs were of monodispersed spherical structure with an average diameter of ∼ 3.7 nm. High-resolution TEM image clearly displayed the lattice spacing of CDs (0.21 nm) which was in accord with the (100) facet of sp2 graphitic carbon. SDS-PAGE electrophoresis and zeta potential measurement were performed to confirm the success in conjugation of PBNPs to Ab2. As shown in Fig. 1E, the molecular weight of Ab2 apparently increased after conjugation with PBNPs. The original zeta potential of PBNPs was − 8.45 mV, while the zeta potential of PBNPs-Ab2 shifts to − 5.69 mv after conjugation with Ab2 (Fig. 1F). These results demonstrated that PBNPs was successfully conjugated with Ab2 via electrostatic interaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of PBNP-mediated ratiometric fluorescence response system
ce:section.ce:para: PBNPs had been proved to own peroxidase-mimicking activity for its ability to catalytically oxidize colorless 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) or 2,2′-azino-bis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) diammonium salt (ABTS) to colored products in the presence of H2O2(Čunderlová et al., 2015; Kim & Lee, 2015; Komkova et al., 2018). However, the catalytic kinetics of PBNPs to OPD, another typical peroxidase substrate, has not been investigated. Thus, the catalytic activity of PBNPs on H2O2-mediated oxidation of OPD had to be verified to assess the feasibility of the proposed ratiometric fluorescence response system. The steady-state kinetics parameters of PBNP were determined by keeping one of the concentrations of OPD and H2O2 invariable and varying the other’s concentration simultaneously. Similar to natural peroxidase, typical Michaelis–Menten curve was observed for PBNPs (Fig. 2 A and B). Line weaver Burk reciprocal plot was performed to obtain the Km and Vmax values (Fig. 2C and D). As displayed in Table S1, the Km value of PBNPs for H2O2 (1.109 mM) was significantly lower than the one of HRP (3.7 mM) (Gao et al., 2007), suggesting that PBNPs exhibits stronger affinity for H2O2 than HRP does. Besides, the Km value of PBNPs for OPD (0.148 mM) was slightly higher than that of HRP (0.135 mM) (Sun, Carr, Li, Lee, McGregor, & Li, 2014), indicating that PBNPs and HRP displayed comparable affinity for OPD.

After verification of the catalytic ability of the prepared PBNPs, further experiments were carried out to validate its capability in mediating the ratiometric fluorescence response. It can be seen from Fig. 3 A, CDs displayed a conspicuous fluorescence emission at 450 nm under the excitation at 400 nm. The sole addition of PBNPs, H2O2 or OPD to CDs solution induced no obvious interference on the fluorescence of CDs. Also, the presence of the mixture of PBNPs/H2O2, PBNPs/OPD, or OPD/H2O2 in CDs solution had negligible effect on the fluorescence of CDs. Nevertheless, with the introduction of PBNPs in the OPD/H2O2/CDs mixture solution, a strong emission peak at 570 nm belonging to the formed oxOPD was observed, and the emission of CDs at 450 nm was remarkably quenched, generating a ratiometric fluorescence response. The quenching mechanism of CDs by oxOPD was further investigated. As displayed in Fig. 3B, the absorption spectrum of oxOPD overlapped with the excitation and emission spectra of CDs to a large extent, implying that quenching of CDs could be resulted from fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or IFE. However, the characteristic absorption peak of CDs exhibited no shift in the absence and presence of oxOPD (Fig. 3C), which signified that no conjugate was formed between CDs and oxOPD, ruling out that FRET occurred between CDs and oxOPD. Additionally, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs/H2O2/OPD in the absence and presence of PBNPs was measured. It can be seen from Fig. 3D that the lifetimes of CDs in mixture of CDs/H2O2/OPD displayed no obvious change after introduction of PBNPs (7.52 ns vs 7.38 ns) further revealing that no energy transfer took place in the system. These results all indicated that the quenching of CDs was mainly attributed to the IFE process between CDs and oxOPD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the ratiometric fluorescence immunoassay
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve the optimal detection performance, several key parameters were optimized including: pH of PBS buffer, incubation time, H2O2 concentration, and OPD concentration. The optimal experimental conditions were as follows: pH 7.0, incubation for 40 min, OPD of 8 mM and 2 H2O2 of 7 mM (Fig. S1). Under the optimized conditions, series concentrations of HA were tested by the fabricated immunoassay, and the ratiometric fluorescence spectra were recorded. As expected, with the increase in concentration of HA from 1.6 ng/mL to125 μg/ mL, the fluorescence intensity of CDs at 450 nm (I450) rose gradually, while the fluorescence intensity of oxOPD at 570 nm (I570) decreased accordingly (Fig. 4 A), and the ratiometric fluorescence value (I570/I450) declined (Fig. 4B). Moreover, the inset of Fig. 4B showed that I570/I450 showed a good linear relationship with the logarithm value of the concentration of HA ranging from 1.6 ng/mL to 125 μg/mL, of which the linear equation was I570/I450 = -1.47 × log C[HA] + 5.71 (R2 = 0.99), with a LOD of 1.2 ng/mL (S/N = 3). Compared with previously reported analytical methods for HA (Table S2), this ratiometric fluorescence immunoassay had comparable or better sensitivity and broader quantification range for HA determination.

Specificity of immunoassay primarily rested on the antibody adopted. This anti-HA monoclonal antibody used in this work showed excellent specificity toward HA as demonstrated by the cross-reactivity (CR) test in our previous study (Luo et al., 2019). Furthermore, the response of this ratiometric fluorescence immunoassay to structural or functional analogs of HA was investigated to confirm its selectivity for HA. As shown in Fig. 5 , the response induced by these analogs were at comparable level with that induced by blank, which was much lower than that induced by HA. Apparently, the proposed ratiometric fluorescence immunoassay had excellent selectivity for HA detection.

Encouraged by the excellent sensitivity and specificity, this ratiometric fluorescence immunoassay was adopted to detect HA in cod fish, red wine, and yogurt samples. As shown in Table 1 , recoveries of 88.54 ∼ 105.96% and a coefficient of variance of 0.71 ∼ 12.6 % for the spiked samples were achieved, indicating the favorable accuracy and precision of this immunoassay. In order to verify the reliability and applicability of this proposed immunoassay, spiked cod fish, red wine and yogurt samples were tested both by the proposed immunoassay and standard ELISA. As shown Fig. S2, results from these two methods exhibited good agreement with each other, further implying the reliability and practicality of the ratiometric fluorescence immunoassay for detection of HA in real samples.

ELISA has become a potent tool for monitoring of food contaminants, which harnesses the enzyme mediated amplification strategy to enable a colorimetric assay. Nevertheless, its robustness and sensitivity will be compromised due to the easy inactivation of enzyme and colorimetric readout. To overcome these drawbacks, PBNPs, as a nanozyme, was used to substitute the HRP to mediate a ratiometric fluorescence immunoassay for accurate and sensitive detection of HA. Owing to the exceptional peroxidase-mimicking activity of PBNPs as well as the inherited accuracy and anti-interference properties of ratio-based fluorometric readout, this proposed immunoassay exhibited superior detection performance over most of the representative methods. In summary, this work not only offered a potent tool for HA contamination monitoring, but also a robust and reliable platform for food safety detection.

Cai-Xia Liao: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Bao-Zhu Jia: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Hong Wang: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Yuan-Ming Sun: Funding acquisition, Project administration. Xiao-Yan Xu: Investigation, Resources. Xiao-Qun Wei: Investigation, Resources. Yu-Dong Shen: Data curation. Hong-Tao Lei: Validation. Zhen-Lin Xu: Funding acquisition, Project administration. Lin Luo: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported Key Area R&D Program of Guangdong Province (2019B020211002), National Natural Science Foundation of China (31822039, 31801621), Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (2021A1515012422), Guangzhou Planned Program in Science and Technology (202102021002).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2021.131907.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
